$comment: x86_64 target descriptor (plan 12 §2.6 ML5 cycle 2 pivot — config-driven targets). Parallels src/dss-config/sources/*.lang.json: a compile target is a config file the LIR substrate reads, NOT C++ code. Adding a new target = drop a new .target.json. Loaded via TargetSchema::loadShipped("x86_64").

target.name: x86_64
target.description: x86_64 opcode set + physical register file + SysV AMD64 / Microsoft x64 calling conventions. Cycle 2b: regfile/ABI added. Cycle 3 (instruction selection) expands the opcode set to full c-subset corpus coverage.
target.$reservedFields: The per-opcode minOperands/maxOperands fields are documented in the JSON for completeness but are NOT yet fully enforced by the substrate. Terminator-ness derives from `terminatorKind` (Br/CondBr/Switch/Return/Unreachable; default `none` = non-terminator); the parser's `parseInst` and `LirVerifier` consult it directly. Per-kind successor-count is enforced by `TargetSchemaData::validate()` via the shared `kTargetTerminatorShapes` table.

$aggregateLayoutComment: FC6 (D-FF3-1): per-ABI struct/union/array layout params the generic type_layout engine reads. `natural` = scalar alignment equals its byte size (capped at maxAlignment) — the rule all four current C ABIs (SysV/Win64/AAPCS64/Apple) share; the format's dataModel supplies the OS-dependent pointer width. maxAlignment 16 = the x86_64 max fundamental alignment (__int128/long double/max_align_t). D-CSUBSET-BITFIELD-ABI-EXACT: `bitFieldStrategy` here is the BACK-COMPAT FALLBACK only — C bit-field packing is OS/format-determined (this CPU serves BOTH ELF-SysV gnu_packed AND PE-MS msvc_straddle), so the ACTIVE strategy is resolved from the object FORMAT (effectiveBitFieldStrategy). The fallback is used only by direct-API / test callers with no format in scope.
$tlsComment: TLS C1 (D-CSUBSET-THREAD-LOCAL): the CPU's static-TLS layout convention — psABI-per-CPU data (x86_64 is Variant II under EVERY OS, so it lives on the TARGET, not the format). variant2 = the thread pointer points ONE PAST the TLS block's end (fs:[0] holds tp itself); a symbol's link-time thread-pointer offset is NEGATIVE: tpoff = templateOffset − alignUp(blockSize, p_align). tcbHeaderBytes = 0 (a Variant-I-only input — arm64 declares 16 with its own block at TLS C2). Consumed ONLY by the walker's tpoff helper when the format's tlsAccess.model is local-exec — a config-keyed formula selector, never a machine-identity branch.

opcodes.$comment: Slot 0 = the Invalid sentinel. Every target must declare it as the first opcode so the substrate's `opcode == 0` check (in LirBuilder::addInst) catches default-constructed insts loudly.

Virtual-ISA pseudo-op: 'this LirReg is the function's k-th input parameter' (payload = k). ML7 calling-convention lowering rewrites `arg` to the real mov-from-arg-register sequence; before that, it represents the parameter as a virtreg that ML5 cycle 3 MIR→LIR isel can consume directly. Every target backend needs SOME representation of 'i-th function parameter'; pre-regalloc this is the uniform shape.

Virtual-ISA pseudo-op (FC7 C1c, D-FC7-SYSV-STRUCT-RETURN-IN-REGS): 'this LirReg is the k-th return register of the immediately-preceding struct-returning call' (payload = per-class return-register ordinal). The caller-side mirror of `arg`. ML7 calling-convention lowering captures it (a mov-from-returnReg, cycle-broken with its sibling pieces) and the ret_piece itself is never encoded. side-effecting so no pass drops/hoists it off its call.

Virtual-ISA pseudo-op (FC7 C3) — callee-side mirror of `arg`: 'this LirReg is the incoming indirect-result (sret) pointer', read from the cc's indirectResultRegister at function entry. Declared for substrate uniformity (like `ret_piece`); x86_64's SysV/ms_x64 conventions pass the sret pointer as a HIDDEN ARG (no indirectResultRegister), so this op is never EMITTED on x86_64 — only register-based sret (AAPCS64/Apple x8) emits it. No encoding (materialized away by lir_callconv).

Virtual-ISA pseudo-op (FC12a-core, D-FC12A-VARIADIC-CALLEE): 'this LirReg is the ADDRESS of the variadic register-save-area' — the frame zone a variadic callee's prologue spills rdi..r9 + the al-gated xmm0..7 into. lir_callconv reserves the zone (sized by the cc's vaListLayout.regSaveAreaBytes) + materializes this op into `lea result, [sp + offset]` once the frame layout is known. The PRESENCE of this op in a function is also lir_callconv's signal that the function called va_start (→ emit the spill). No encoding (materialized away).

Virtual-ISA pseudo-op (FC12a-core, D-FC12A-VARIADIC-CALLEE): 'this LirReg is the ADDRESS of the incoming overflow stack args' — where varargs that didn't fit in registers live (the caller's outgoing-args area, above this frame). lir_callconv materializes it into `lea result, [sp + totalFrameSize + callPushBytes + shadowSpaceBytes]` (the same geometry the stack-resident `arg` read uses; on SysV shadowSpaceBytes=0 so it is `totalFrameSize + callPushBytes`). NOTE (FC12b): this offset's `totalFrameSize + callPushBytes` base ALSO serves as the Win64 va_home_arg_area home base, which must NOT include shadowSpaceBytes — the Win64 home leaf is a SEPARATE op (`va_home_arg_area`) so this op may keep its shadow term; do not collapse them. No encoding (materialized away).

Virtual-ISA pseudo-op (D-FC12-VARIADIC-OVERFLOW-FIXED-AGGREGATE-STACK-ARGS): callee-side mirror of the caller's `ByValueStackArg` carrier — 'this LirReg is the ADDRESS of a FIXED by-value aggregate param that was placed WHOLLY on the incoming stack' (it straddled the reg/stack boundary, all-or-nothing). lir_callconv materializes it into `lea result, [sp + totalFrameSize + callPushBytes + shadowSpaceBytes + payload]` — IDENTICAL geometry to va_overflow_arg_area but the PAYLOAD is THIS aggregate's byte offset WITHIN the incoming overflow area (0 when it is the first/only overflowed fixed param). HIR→MIR then byte-copies from this address into the param's local slot. DISTINCT from the va_* ops on purpose: it must NOT be one of lir_callconv's va_start-detection signals (it occurs in NON-variadic functions too — reusing va_overflow_arg_area would falsely trigger the variadic prologue spill). No encoding (materialized away).

Virtual-ISA pseudo-op (c69, D-CSUBSET-ALLOCA-ADDRESS-REMATERIALIZE): a RE-REFERENCE of a body-local's stack slot — 'this fresh LirReg is the ADDRESS of local-alloca #k', where k = PAYLOAD = the alloca's 0-based scan-order index within the function. The c69 entry-block alloca-hoist makes every local's address live from function entry; reusing ONE address vreg across that range tips a register-pressure cliff, so MIR->LIR emits the `alloca` op ONCE (it reserves the slot) and a fresh `lea_frame_slot k` at EACH use of the address — each result has a tiny def->use range, the conventional 'rematerialize frame addresses' treatment. lir_callconv materializes it into `lea result, [sp + localAreaOffset + offsetOf(k) + disp]` — byte-identical to the `lea` the original alloca (same k) resolves to (offset read from the alloca-index->offset map lir_callconv builds in scan order). An OPTIONAL single ImmInt operand is a folded CONSTANT byte displacement `disp` (a `&local[constIdx]`/`&s.field` Gep whose base IS this alloca, folded by lowerGep into ONE sp-based frame lea) — this is cheaper (one lea) AND keeps the arm64 >16 MiB MOVZ/MOVK lea macro safe (base = sp, never the result reg, so `MOVZ Xd;ADD Xd,sp,Xd` never clobbers the base). DISTINCT from `alloca` so it is INVISIBLE to the frame-layout slot-count scan (`functionLocalAllocaPayloads` counts only `alloca`): the frame still reserves exactly ONE slot per original alloca. No encoding (materialized away). hasSideEffects=false (a pure address computation, like `lea`/`alloca`); no LIR-tier pass removes/merges it (the pipeline runs no LIR DCE/CSE), so its result-used ranges always survive to materialization.

Virtual-ISA pseudo-op (c116 H1, D-WIN64-SEH-FUNCLETS): 'this fresh LirReg is the ADDRESS of local-slot #k IN THE FRAME the operand[0] register establishes' — the generic analog of LLVM's llvm.localrecover. operand[0] = an establisher-frame base pointer register; PAYLOAD = k = the 0-based scan-order index of a local in that frame. lir_callconv materializes it into `lea result, [establisherReg + parentLocalAreaOffset(k)]`, where parentLocalAreaOffset comes from the config FrameLayout of the frame the base establishes (the SAME localAreaOffset()+k*slotSize geometry `alloca`/`lea_frame_slot` use, but based off the establisher REGISTER rather than SP, and against the PARENT function's finalized layout — lir_callconv threads sibling layouts + the funclet->parent map to resolve it). The c116 SEH filter FUNCLET is its first consumer: a __except filter reading a parent local (sqlite's `pWal`) recovers it via `[establisher + parentSlotOffset]`, since a fault-time x64 establisher frame (FrameRegister=0) == the parent's post-prologue SP. AGNOSTIC: base is a normal SSA operand (never a hardcoded rdx), the offset is from FrameLayout, and the op names nothing arch/format/SEH-specific. No encoding (materialized away). hasSideEffects=false (a pure address computation, like `lea`/`alloca`/`lea_frame_slot`).

Virtual-ISA pseudo-op (FC12b, D-FC12B-WIN64-VARIADIC-CALLEE): 'this LirReg is the ADDRESS of the Win64 HomogeneousPointer va_start base' — &home[namedArgCount], the first vararg. Win64's named-arg home space (rcx/rdx/r8/r9 slots) and the 5th+ stack args are CONTIGUOUS in the caller's outgoing area, so this single base + the named-arg slot count positions `ap` past ALL named args. PAYLOAD = the named-arg slot count; lir_callconv materializes it into `lea result, [sp + totalFrameSize + callPushBytes + payload*outgoingSlotSize]` — the NO-SHADOW base (the home space IS the caller's shadow space, so adding shadowSpaceBytes would skip it and read past every named arg). Its PRESENCE is also lir_callconv's signal to spill the named integer arg regs into their home slots. No encoding (materialized away).

2-operand-shape mov: destination = result vreg, source = operands[0]. AS2 cycle 2 wires this to two encoding variants: REX.W 0x8B /r (mov r64, r64) when source is a register; REX.W 0xC7 /0 imm32 (mov r/m64, imm32 SIGN-extended to 64-bit) when source is an immediate. FC3 c2 (D-CSUBSET-32BIT-ALU-FORMS): both shapes gain explicit `width` keys + 32-bit siblings (same opcodes WITHOUT REX.W, Intel SDM Vol. 2B MOV) — a 32-bit register write auto-zero-extends to the full 64-bit register, and the 32-bit imm32 form is EXACT (not sign-extending), the difference that matters for an `unsigned int` 0xFFFFFFFF. Plumbing movs (phi/copy/capture/spill) are emitted width-default (64) — full-register copies are value-correct for every type. FC8 (D-CSUBSET-BITFIELD-WIDE-UNIT): a fifth variant materializes a FULL 64-bit immediate — REX.W B8+rd io (mov r64, imm64) — selected by the `imm64` operand-kind (a `LiteralIndex` LIR operand: the wide value lives in the LIR literal pool, since it does not fit the inline imm32 path). This is the ONLY x86-64 form that loads a >imm32 constant into a register; its destination rides the opcode byte (B8+rd), not ModR/M, so it uses the `opcode.reg` result slot.

variants.$comment: mov r32, r32 — 0x8B /r without REX.W (zero-extends to r64).
mov r/m32, imm32 — 0xC7 /0 without REX.W: writes the EXACT 32-bit immediate + zero upper (the 64-bit form sign-extends).

FC8 (D-CSUBSET-BITFIELD-WIDE-UNIT): mov r64, imm64 — REX.W (0x48) + B8+rd + 8-byte LE immediate (Intel SDM Vol. 2B MOV, REX.W B8+rd io). The destination register's low 3 bits ride the opcode byte (B8+rd) via the `opcode.reg` result slot; its high bit drives REX.B. The `imm64` operand-kind is a `LirOperand{kind==LiteralIndex}` — the wide value lives in the LIR literal pool (the 8-byte operand POD cannot inline a 64-bit immediate), and the encoder reads it back to emit the 8 LE bytes of the `io` field. width:64 keys it to 64-bit constants only; there is no 32-bit imm64 form (a 32-bit-typed wide constant uses the imm32 forms above).

opcodes.$comment: add reg-reg — REX.W 0x01 /r (ADD r/m64, r64). LIR is 3-address (dst, src1, src2); requires2Address=true makes the legalize pass insert `mov dst, src1` first when dst!=src1, then the assembler emits `add dst, src2` (writing into ModR/M.rm, which is dst).

encoding.$comment: 2-address shape: `operands[0]` IS the destination after `lir_2addr_legalize` ensures result==operands[0]. So no resultSlot — the wire for operands[0] handles the destination placement (its hwEncoding goes into ModR/M.rm). validate() rule G has a `requires2Address` exception for exactly this shape. FC3 c2: width-keyed 64/32 pairs — the 32-bit forms are the SAME opcodes without REX.W (Intel SDM ADD), computing mod-2^32 with auto-zero-extension (the C-defined `unsigned int` wraparound).

variants.$comment: add r/m32, r32 — 0x01 /r without REX.W (32-bit add, zero-extends).

add reg-imm32 — REX.W 0x81 /0 imm32 (ADD r/m64, imm32). Used by ML7 epilogue's `add rsp, N` stack-frame teardown. 2-address shape: operands[0] is dest, imm32 is immediate. modrmRegExt=0 fills ModR/M.reg with the opcode-extension digit (/0 for ADD).
add r/m32, imm32 — 0x81 /0 without REX.W.

opcodes.$comment: sub reg-reg — REX.W 0x29 /r (SUB r/m64, r64). Same 2-address shape as `add`.

variants.$comment: FC3 c2: sub r/m32, r32 — 0x29 /r without REX.W.

sub reg-imm32 — REX.W 0x81 /5 imm32 (SUB r/m64, imm32). Used by ML7 prologue's `sub rsp, N` stack-frame setup. 2-address shape (operands[0] is dest, imm32 is immediate). modrmRegExt=5 fills ModR/M.reg with the opcode-extension digit (/5 for SUB).
FC3 c2: sub r/m32, imm32 — 0x81 /5 without REX.W.

opcodes.$comment: D-CSUBSET-VLA (C1b): `sub_sp_reg sp, <sizeReg>` — subtract a RUNTIME byte count (in a register) from the stack pointer, the dynamic-alloca core (`int a[n]` => `sub rsp, alignUp(n*4,16)`). REX.W 0x29 /r (SUB r/m64, r64), the SAME opcode as `sub` reg-reg but a DISTINCT mnemonic (its own single encoding: operand0=sp is BOTH the r/m destination AND source1, operand1=size is the r64 source2). result:none (SP mutates in place — no SSA value; the moved SP is captured by a following `sp_copy`). hasSideEffects=true so no pass drops/reorders it relative to the sp_copy that captures the result. Emitted by MIR->LIR (with the physical stack-pointer operand); the callconv pass passes it through unchanged.

D-CSUBSET-VLA (C1b): `sp_copy dst, src` — a plain register move used for the three frame-pointer plumbing roles a VLA function needs: prologue FP<-SP (capture the fixed-frame base), epilogue SP<-FP (discard the VLA + restore SP to the fixed-frame base), and the VLA-base capture base<-SP (the post-`sub_sp_reg` stack pointer). REX.W 0x8B /r (MOV r64, r/m64), a DISTINCT mnemonic from `mov` so it can be marked hasSideEffects=true (it reads/writes the physical SP; no pass may drop or reorder it across the `sub_sp_reg`). resultSlot modrm.reg = dst; operand0=src -> modrm.rm. rsp/rbp are valid register-direct in both fields.

D-WIN64-LARGE-FRAME-STACK-PROBE: a SINGLE LIR op that the x86_64 encoder lowers to a MULTI-INSTRUCTION inline stack-probe loop (one-LIR-op→N-instructions). Operands: [sp(reg), frameBytes(imm32), pageBytes(imm32)] — the stack-pointer register, the total frame size to descend, and the guard-page step (= the cc's stackProbePageBytes). The ML7 prologue emits this INSTEAD of a bare `sub rsp, F` when totalFrameSize > stackProbePageBytes, so the loop touches (commits) every guard page on the way down (Windows reserves the stack lazily behind a single PAGE_GUARD page; a bare `sub` skips it → access violation). The encoder hand-pushes the loop: `mov r11d, frame` / .L: `sub rsp, page` / `or qword [rsp], 0` (page-touch) / `sub r11d, page` / `cmp r11d, page` / `ja .L` (rel8 back) / `sub rsp, r11` (final remainder). r11 is ms_x64 caller-saved, never an arg/return reg, and the probe runs before any arg-home, so it is free. NO __chkstk, NO relocation (the rel8 is a compile-time constant). This is x86_64-ONLY: arm64 OMITS this opcode entirely (its CCs have stackProbePageBytes=0 → never emitted → arm64 encoder would fire A_NoEncodingDeclared, defense-in-depth). The encoding block below exists ONLY to satisfy the shape/routing contract (shape!=None ⇒ asm.cpp routes to the x86-variable walker; the walker's DEDICATED stack_probe arm intercepts BEFORE variant matching and hand-emits the bytes — the variant row itself is never consumed for emission). hasSideEffects=true so no pass drops/reorders it.

variants.$comment: Contract-only variant (see opcode $comment): the x86-variable walker's DEDICATED stack_probe arm emits the probe loop by hand BEFORE this variant is ever matched, so this row carries NO operand wiring (an empty guard — the schema's per-position wire check requires a wire for every declared operandKind, but the probe op's 3 operands are consumed by the hand-written arm, not by slot wiring). Its sole purpose is to make `encoding.shape != None` so asm.cpp routes stack_probe to the x86-variable walker (and opcodeInfo != nullptr, avoiding A_NoEncodingDeclared). If the dedicated arm ever fails to intercept (mnemonic drift), this empty-guard variant does NOT match the 3-operand inst -> A_NoMatchingEncodingVariant fires LOUD (never a silent mis-encode). `template.opcode` is a placeholder never emitted.

opcodes.$comment: 2-operand multiply. x86 has both 2-op `imul reg, reg` (writes the low half to the destination — what we emit) and 1-op `mul/imul reg` (rdx:rax full-width — needed only for overflow/128-bit results, future cycle). The 2-op form is REX.W 0x0F 0xAF /r — UNUSUAL among x86 binary arith because dest goes to ModR/M.reg (not r/m), source-1 goes to r/m. Wires reflect this: op[0]→modrm.reg (the destination per 2-address), op[1]→modrm.rm.

variants.$comment: FC3 c2: imul r32, r/m32 — 0F AF /r without REX.W (32-bit multiply, low half, zero-extends; the C-defined unsigned-int product mod 2^32).

opcodes.$comment: D-CSUBSET-DIVISION-OP-CODEGEN — CQO sign-extend (cycle 10r split, 2026-06-04). Sign-extends RAX into RDX:RAX as the pre-divide setup for signed division. REX.W 0x99 = 0x48 0x99 (2 bytes, no ModR/M). **Cycle 10r REX-overlap bug fix**: cycle 10q packaged CQO+IDIV as a single compound opcode with `opcode: [72, 153, 72, 247]`. But the encoder auto-emits one REX prefix at the start (e.g., 0x41 for REX.B when the operand is R14) — per x86 decode the LAST REX prefix before the opcode wins, so the embedded literal second 0x48 (REX.W only) superseded the auto-REX 0x41, losing REX.B for the IDIV operand. Result: IDIV reads `modrm.rm low 3 bits` as RSI (without REX.B) instead of R14, dividing by garbage — STATUS_INTEGER_DIVIDE_BY_ZERO trap. Splitting CQO into its own opcode lets each instruction get its own auto-REX correctly. implicitRegisters: inputs=[rax] (CQO reads RAX); outputs=[rdx] (CQO writes the sign-extended high half); clobbered=[rdx] (RDX's prior value is destroyed).

variants.$comment: FC3 c2: the 32-bit sibling is CDQ — the SAME 0x99 opcode WITHOUT REX.W (Intel SDM CWD/CDQ/CQO: operand size selects which) — sign-extends EAX into EDX:EAX, the pre-divide setup the 32-bit IDIV consumes. The MIR→LIR div lowering threads ONE width through the pre+core pair so they always agree.

opcodes.$comment: D-CSUBSET-DIVISION-OP-CODEGEN — signed-divide single op (cycle 10r split). IDIV r/m64 divides RDX:RAX by operand 0 (modrm.rm), placing quotient in RAX + remainder in RDX. Encoder auto-computes REX with W=1 + B=(operand 0's high bit), correctly handling R8-R15 operands. Cycle 10r split from the prior compound encoding to fix REX-prefix overlap silent-miscompile (see cqo $comment). implicitRegisters: inputs=[rax,rdx] (IDIV reads RDX:RAX as 128-bit dividend); outputs=[rax,rdx] (quotient + remainder); clobbered=[rax,rdx] (both prior values destroyed — required by the schema-loader `outputs ⊆ clobbered` invariant added in the 10r 7-agent review fold, since regalloc consumes `clobbered` to build its forbidden set). inputRoles/outputRoles (D-CSUBSET-MOD-OP-CODEGEN-OUTPUT-INDEX-CONTRACT, 2026-06-10): the MIR→LIR div/mod lowering pins the dividend + captures the quotient (SDiv) or remainder (SMod) BY ROLE — a reorder of the positional arrays can no longer silently flip the projected register.

variants.$comment: FC3 c2: idiv r/m32 — 0xF7 /7 without REX.W (divides EDX:EAX, quotient EAX / remainder EDX, both zero-extending into the full registers). Pairs with the width-32 cqo sibling (CDQ).

opcodes.$comment: D-CSUBSET-DIVISION-OP-CODEGEN — XOR EDX, EDX as the pre-divide zero-extend for unsigned divide (cycle 10r split). 0x31 0xD2 — 32-bit XOR with explicit dest/src both = EDX. 32-bit op zero-extends to RDX, clearing all 64 bits. No REX prefix (32-bit operands). FLAG 1 silent-miscompile guard at byte-level: the unsigned-divide pre-emit uses ZERO-extend (this op); signed-divide uses SIGN-extend (cqo). implicitRegisters: outputs=[rdx]; clobbered=[rdx]. No inputs declared (the op writes a constant zero, doesn't read the prior RDX value).

variants.$comment: FC3 c2: deliberately WIDTH-ABSENT (match-any). XOR EDX,EDX is ALREADY a 32-bit op that zeroes the full RDX — the same bytes are the correct unsigned-divide pre-op at BOTH widths (the 32-bit DIV reads only EDX of it).

opcodes.$comment: D-CSUBSET-DIVISION-OP-CODEGEN — unsigned-divide single op (cycle 10r split). DIV r/m64 divides RDX:RAX by operand 0 (modrm.rm), placing quotient in RAX + remainder in RDX. Encoder auto-computes REX with W=1 + B=(operand 0's high bit). implicitRegisters: inputs=[rax,rdx]; outputs=[rax,rdx]; clobbered=[rax,rdx] (mirrors `idiv_op` — required by the `outputs ⊆ clobbered` invariant). inputRoles/outputRoles mirror `idiv_op` (D-CSUBSET-MOD-OP-CODEGEN-OUTPUT-INDEX-CONTRACT): UDiv captures role 'quotient', UMod captures role 'remainder'.

variants.$comment: FC3 c2: div r/m32 — 0xF7 /6 without REX.W (unsigned 32-bit divide of EDX:EAX; quotient EAX zero-extends).

opcodes.$comment: c103 (D-CSUBSET-INTRINSIC-UMULH) — unsigned MUL-HIGH single op. MUL r/m64 (0xF7 /4) multiplies RAX by operand 0 (modrm.rm), placing the 128-bit product's low half in RAX and the HIGH half in RDX. The __umulh intrinsic captures the RDX 'high' outputRole (the RAX low half is discarded). NO pre-op — unlike div's cqo, MUL overwrites RDX:RAX unconditionally, so RDX is output-only and NOT an implicit input (contrast div_op inputs=[rax,rdx]). implicitRegisters honour outputs ⊆ clobbered: inputs=[rax]; outputs/clobbered=[rax,rdx]; inputRoles multiplicand=rax; outputRoles high=rdx (+ low=rax for completeness — the MIR->LIR lowering reads only 'high'). Encoder auto-computes REX with W=1 + B=(operand 0's high bit).

variants.$comment: MUL r/m32 — 0xF7 /4 without REX.W (unsigned 32-bit multiply of EAX; high half in EDX). Present for completeness; the u64 __umulh never selects it.

opcodes.$comment: c104 (D-CSUBSET-INTRINSIC-ATOMIC-CAS) — atomic compare-and-swap, mem-dest: LOCK CMPXCHG [base+disp32], reg (LOCK 0xF0 mandatoryPrefix + 0x0F 0xB1 /r). Semantics: compares RAX/EAX (the implicit 'comparand' input-role) with [mem]; equal -> stores the wired reg (the NEW value, modrm.reg); unequal -> loads [mem] into RAX/EAX. EITHER way RAX ends holding the ORIGINAL observed value (the 'old' output-role the MIR AtomicCas lowering captures). LOCK makes it a full barrier — the correct strength for Win32 InterlockedCompareExchange. Operand shape mirrors `store`'s 4-op mem-dest form: [newval_reg, base_reg, MemBase(scale), MemOffset(disp)]. mandatoryPrefix [240] emits BEFORE the auto-REX (the 0x66 width-16-store precedent — legacy prefixes precede REX, REX immediately precedes the opcode), so a high-numbered base/newval register REX.B/R composes correctly: F0 [REX] 0F B1 /r. Width 32 = the Win32 LONG (LLP64) CAS — NO rexW; width 64 for completeness (a future 64-bit CAS consumer). implicitRegisters: inputs=[rax] (the comparand), outputs/clobbered=[rax] (the observed-original; outputs ⊆ clobbered).

variants.$comment: 64-bit form (REX.W): lock cmpxchg qword [base+disp32], r64 — no shipped consumer yet (InterlockedCompareExchange is the 32-bit LONG CAS); declared so a future i64 CAS selects it by width instead of silently mis-encoding.

opcodes.$comment: D-CSUBSET-WHILE-LOOP-SUBSTRATE (step 13.5 cycle 1, 2026-06-03): integer compare (writes FLAGS, no GPR result). Two variants: reg-reg (REX.W 0x39 /r — cmp r/m64, r64) + reg-imm32 (REX.W 0x81 /7 imm32 — cmp r/m64, imm32 with sign-extended imm32). modrmRegExt=7 fills ModR/M.reg with the /7 opcode-extension digit. ML5 lowerICmp + lowerCondBr both emit `cmp` against an immediate-0 or against another reg.

variants.$comment: FC3 c2: cmp r/m32, r32 — 0x39 /r without REX.W. A U32 compare MUST read 32 bits: a bitcast-from-negative-int operand carries sign-extended upper bits a 64-wide compare would misread.
FC3 c2: cmp r/m32, imm32 — 0x81 /7 without REX.W (the lowerCondBr nonzero-test on a 32-bit-typed condition).

opcodes.$comment: D-CSUBSET-WHILE-LOOP-SUBSTRATE (step 13.5 cycle 1, 2026-06-03): materialize 0/1 byte from FLAGS by cond-code. Encoding `0F 90+cc /0 r8` — the LOW 4 BITS of the SECOND opcode byte carry the condition (resolved via the target's `condCodeEncoding` table from the inst's payload). Result is r8 (low byte of a GPR); upper bits left undefined per Intel SDM. The LIR substrate treats setcc's result as a GPR vreg — callers either compare against 0 immediately (cmp r64, imm32 with sign-extend; safe for boolean compare since AL=0/1) or zero-extend via a follow-up movzx. Cycle scope keeps the substrate minimal; D-LIR-SETCC-MOVZX-COMPOUND anchors the zero-extend pairing when a boolean must escape into a fully-extended 64-bit slot.

variants.$templateComment: D-LIR-SETCC-WIDTH-CONTRACT (step 13.5 cycle 1 post-fold, code-reviewer C2): `forceRexPrefix: true` forces a REX prefix even when no W/R/X/B bit is set. Required so the byte-register-bearing setcc accesses spl/bpl/sil/dil (low byte of r4..r7) instead of the legacy ah/ch/dh/bh high-byte aliases. Without this, regalloc picking rsp/rbp/rsi/rdi would silently write the setcc result into the HIGH byte of a DIFFERENT physical register.

opcodes.$comment: Hard-trap / 'this code path is unreachable'. AS1 maps to x86_64 ud2 (encoding `0F 0B`). Encoding added at D-LK10-ENTRY Slice A (2026-06-02) so the trampoline emitter can append `unreachable` after noreturn syscall / call_indirect_via_extern call sites — reaches `assemble()` and must emit bytes (the `TargetEncodingShape::None` arm in `asm.cpp` fails loud `A_NoEncodingDeclared`). Shape mirrors the `ret` opcode (zero-operand variant, no ModR/M, opcode bytes literal).

D-LK10-ENTRY Slice A: raw kernel transition (Linux exit_group / Mach-O BSD exit). Zero-operand, no result, no successors, NOT a terminator (general-case opcode: FF6 hello-world's `write` syscall returns to the next instruction; only the trampoline's `exit` syscall is noreturn, and that's signaled by the trampoline emitter appending a separate `unreachable` after). Encoding: x86_64 SYSCALL `0F 05` — fixed 2-byte opcode, same shape vocabulary as `ret` and the new `unreachable`. The syscall-number register is loaded into `rax` (or per the target's `syscallNumGpr` field on format schema's `processExit` — Slice B) by a preceding LIR `mov` op, NOT a payload on `syscall` itself.

D-LK10-ENTRY Slice A: indirect call through an extern-import IAT slot (Windows kernel32!ExitProcess, future macOS libSystem imports). x86_64 encoding `FF 15 disp32` (call qword ptr [RIP + disp32]) — the same 4-byte disp32 + REL32 reloc shape as the existing direct `call` opcode (E8 disp32), just with a 2-byte opcode literal `[255, 21]` (FF + ModR/M byte 15h = mod=00 reg=/2 rm=101 → [RIP+disp32]). NOT a terminator (general-case indirect call; the trampoline emitter appends `unreachable` after when the callee is noreturn like ExitProcess). 1 operand = ExternImport SymbolRef. The `rel32` reloc-kind row (in this file's `relocations[]` array) computes `S - P - 4` which is exactly what `[RIP+disp32]` resolution needs at load time (S = IAT slot VA, P = the disp32 patch site, RIP at execution = patch + 4). D-LK10-ENTRY-ML7-FRAME-BIAS-UNIFY post-fold (2026-06-02): result widened from `none` to `optional` — same shape as `call` — so MIR→LIR's `lowerCall` can emit `call_indirect_via_extern` for an extern returning a non-void value (puts returns int). The materialize pass moves the return register into the result vreg uniformly for both `call` and `call_indirect_via_extern`; the schema's result-rule must permit both void and non-void targets.

D-CSUBSET-WHILE-LOOP-SUBSTRATE (step 13.5 cycle 1, 2026-06-03): unconditional intra-function jump. Encoding `E9 rel32` (5 bytes). The 4-byte trailing rel32 is resolved at assemble-time via the BlockRel32 slot + per-function patch list (NOT a linker relocation — intra-function only). Operand[0] is a BlockRef naming the target basic block.

D-CSUBSET-COMPUTED-GOTO: indirect branch `jmp r/m64` — 0xFF /4 (jmp rax = FF E0). Mirrors `call`'s 0xFF /2 indirect form: NO REX.W (FF /4 defaults to 64-bit in long mode); REX.B derives from the register's high ordinal bit. Operand[0] is the target-address register; successors = all address-taken blocks (terminatorKind indirect-br, variadic successors). The MIR IndirectBr lowers to this; the address register is materialized from a preceding `lea` of the target block's synthetic symbol (BlockAddress).

D-CSUBSET-WHILE-LOOP-SUBSTRATE (step 13.5 cycle 1, 2026-06-03): conditional intra-function jump with mandatory fallthrough. Compound encoding `0F 8x rel32; E9 rel32` (11 bytes total). The cond nibble in 0x80's low 4 bits comes from the inst's payload (TargetCondCode) via the schema's condCodeEncoding table. Both target blocks pass as BlockRef operands: operand[0] = ifTrue (taken when cond holds — encoded as the first rel32); operand[1] = ifFalse (the fallthrough — encoded as the trailing `E9 rel32`). The wire-1's `prefixOpcodeBytes: [0xE9]` bridges the two displacements. A future optimizer pass elides the trailing jmp when ifFalse is the next-laid-out block (anchored D-OPT-JCC-FALLTHROUGH).

call — direct AND indirect near call, one mnemonic with per-callee-kind variants (FC4 c2). Direct `call sym`: 0xE8 + 4-byte signed PC-relative displacement (filled with 0 by the assembler; the linker patches via the rel32 relocation). Indirect `call reg` (function-pointer callee): 0xFF /2 with the callee register in ModR/M.rm — NO REX.W (FF /2 defaults to 64-bit operand size in long mode); REX.B derives automatically from the register's high ordinal bit (call r10 = 41 FF D2). Keeping both under ONE mnemonic (a variant, not a second opcode) keeps isCall / functionHasCalls / caller-saved-clobber semantics shared by construction. The schema's maxOperands=255 admits a call ABI where extra operands carry arg-register hints; the callconv materializer emits the single-operand forms.

variants.$comment: FC4 c2: call r/m64 — 0xFF /2 (indirect call through a register; call rax = FF D0). No width key: FF /2 is 64-bit by default in long mode, so the variant matches any declared operation width. /digit precedent: idiv /7, div /6, shl /4.

opcodes.$comment: ret — 0xC3 near-return. The schema's maxOperands=1 admits an optional return value carrier; AS2 cycle 2 wires the zero-operand variant only. The actual return value is materialized into the calling-convention's return register by ML7 callconv-lowering BEFORE the ret instruction is emitted (via a `mov` to rax/xmm0); the ret instruction itself never carries an operand at the byte level.

variants.$comment: ret — no operands at the LIR level. ML7 cycle 2's callee-side return-value materialization moves the MIR Return's vreg into cc.returnGprs[0]/returnFprs[0] BEFORE the ret, so the assembler sees a 0-operand ret form.

opcodes.$comment: Allocate a stack-frame slot (cycle 3c). Cycle 3c is the pass-through layer: payload is threaded verbatim from MIR Alloca (currently 0 — MIR does not yet encode size/align; that lands when ML6's frame-layout phase pulls on it). Result is a pointer-class virtreg holding the slot's effective address. AS1 turns this into a frame-relative `lea` once the frame layout is known.

Load from memory. Operands: [base_reg, MemBase(scale=1), MemOffset(disp)]. Optional index-reg arm added in cycle 3d. Emits `mov reg, [base + disp]`: REX.W 0x8B /r ModR/M(mod=10, reg=result, rm=base) [SIB 0x24 when base.lo3==4] disp32. Plan 13 §3.1 D-AS4-1 — landed alongside ML7 cycle 2 to close D-LK10-2 byte-on-disk path.

variants.$comment: 3-op no-index form, width 64: `mov reg, [base + disp32]` (REX.W 0x8B). Width-keyed (was width-absent) now that the byte form below is declared — convergence-fix D forbids mixing a match-any sibling with a width-keyed one of the SAME operandKinds. Every GPR integer/pointer load carries width 64 (memAccessWidthFlags returns 0 ⇒ width 64 for non-Char GPR types — full-slot read, value-correct since loaded ints are consumed low-bits-only).

3-op no-index form, width 8 (D-CSUBSET-CHAR-STRING-VALUE-CODEGEN): `movzx reg, byte [base + disp32]` (REX.W 0F B6 /r). A char/byte memory access MUST be byte-EXACT — a 64-bit read of a 1-byte string/array element reads 7 bytes past it (a fault at a section/page edge). ZERO-extend (movzx) is deliberate: the load is a SIGNEDNESS-AGNOSTIC byte fetch — sign semantics live in the explicit cast that follows (SExt for signed `char`, ZExt for unsigned `byte`), each of which re-reads the low byte, so the load's upper bits never reach a consumer un-recast (C integer-promotion always widens a char operand before arithmetic). REX.W sets the r64 dest (its low 8 hold the byte); the memory source has no high-byte-alias hazard (that is a register-source-only concern). Selected when the Load's result type is Char (width 8 on the LIR inst, via memAccessWidthFlags).

3-op no-index form, width 32 (D-LIR-INT-MEMORY-WIDTH-EXACT): `mov r32, [base + disp32]` (0x8B WITHOUT REX.W). A 4-byte int load MUST be width-exact — a 64-bit read of a PACKED int (an array element / struct field, NOT a stand-alone scalar local) reads 4 bytes past it (the store peer clobbers the neighbour / overruns the frame slot — the bug the array-storage cycle exposed). A 32-bit register write auto-zero-extends bits 63:32 (Intel SDM MOV), so the int lands clean in the full register. Selected when the Load's result type is a 4-byte integer (I32/U32, or `long` under LLP64) — width 32 via memAccessWidthFlags.

3-op no-index form, width 16 (D-LIR-INT-MEMORY-WIDTH-EXACT): `movzx r32, word [base + disp32]` (0F B7 /r, no REX.W). A 2-byte short load must be half-word-exact — a wider read of a packed short reads past it. ZERO-extend (movzx) is the signedness-agnostic half-word fetch (the width-8 char movzx peer at 0F B6); the short→int promote cast that follows re-reads the low 16 bits (SExt/ZExt-16 = D-CSUBSET-SUBNATIVE-ALU-FORMS, not yet encoded ⇒ short VALUE use fails loud, never silently miscompiles). Selected when the Load's result type is I16/U16 — width 16 via memAccessWidthFlags.
4-op indexed form (D-AS4-5), width 64: `mov reg, [base + index*scale + disp32]` (REX.W 0x8B). The walker emits SIB unconditionally when SibIndex wires.

4-op indexed form, width 8 (D-CSUBSET-CHAR-STRING-VALUE-CODEGEN): `movzx reg, byte [base + index*scale + disp32]` (REX.W 0F B6 /r + SIB). The byte-exact + signedness-agnostic rationale is the 3-op width-8 form's; this arm serves a runtime-index char-array access (`arr[i]`, scale 1 since sizeof(char)==1).

opcodes.$comment: Store to memory. Operands: [value_reg, base_reg, MemBase(scale), MemOffset(disp)] OR [value_reg, base_reg, index_reg, MemBase(scale), MemOffset(disp)]. No result. Emits `mov [base (+ index*scale) + disp32], reg`: REX.W 0x89 /r ModR/M(mod=10, reg=value, rm=base) [SIB] disp32. Plan 13 §3.1 D-AS4-1 + D-AS4-5.

variants.$comment: 4-op no-index form (D-AS4-1), width 64: `mov [base + disp32], reg` (REX.W 0x89). Width-keyed (was width-absent) now that the byte form below is declared — convergence-fix D forbids mixing a match-any sibling with a width-keyed one of the SAME operandKinds. Every GPR integer/pointer store carries width 64 (memAccessWidthFlags returns 0 ⇒ width 64 for non-Char GPR types).

4-op no-index form, width 8 (D-CSUBSET-CHAR-STRING-VALUE-CODEGEN): `mov byte [base + disp32], reg8` (0x88 /r). A char/byte store writes EXACTLY the low byte — a 64-bit store of a 1-byte element would clobber 7 bytes past it. `forceRexPrefix: true` (NOT rexW — a byte op) forces a REX prefix so the stored VALUE register (wired to modrm.reg) addresses spl/bpl/sil/dil (low byte of r4..r7) instead of the legacy ah/ch/dh/bh high-byte aliases — exactly the setcc byte-register rationale. Signedness-agnostic: only the low 8 bits are written. Selected when the stored value's type is Char (width 8 via memAccessWidthFlags).

4-op no-index form, width 32 (D-LIR-INT-MEMORY-WIDTH-EXACT): `mov [base + disp32], r32` (0x89 WITHOUT REX.W). A 4-byte int store MUST write EXACTLY 4 bytes — a 64-bit store of a PACKED int element clobbers 4 bytes of the neighbour and overruns the frame slot (the array-storage crash this cycle fixes). Selected when the stored value's type is a 4-byte integer (I32/U32, or `long` under LLP64) — width 32 via memAccessWidthFlags.

4-op no-index form, width 16 (D-LIR-INT-MEMORY-WIDTH-EXACT): `mov [base + disp32], r16` (0x66 0x89 — the 0x66 operand-size prefix selects 16-bit, emitted before any REX). Writes EXACTLY 2 bytes — a wider store of a packed short clobbers its neighbour. No forceRexPrefix (a 16-bit store has no high-byte-alias hazard — that is a byte-register concern). Selected when the stored value's type is I16/U16 — width 16 via memAccessWidthFlags.
5-op indexed form (D-AS4-5), width 64: `mov [base + index*scale + disp32], reg` (REX.W 0x89 + SIB).

5-op indexed form, width 8 (D-CSUBSET-CHAR-STRING-VALUE-CODEGEN): `mov byte [base + index*scale + disp32], reg8` (0x88 /r + SIB + forceRexPrefix). Byte-exact + forceRexPrefix rationale is the 4-op width-8 form's; this arm serves a runtime-index char-array store (`arr[i] = c`, scale 1).

opcodes.$comment: TLS C1 (D-CSUBSET-THREAD-LOCAL): materialize the current thread's TLS base pointer (tp) into a GPR — x86 form `mov r64, seg:[disp32]` = <segByte> 48 8B ModRM(00,reg,100) SIB(0x25) disp32. EVERY format-specific VALUE is config, threaded by the lowering: the segment-override byte (0x64 fs on ELF-Linux / 0x65 gs on PE) rides the LIR PAYLOAD via the template's payloadBytePrefix (emitted as the instruction's FIRST byte — prefix group 2 precedes REX); the tp-slot displacement (fs:[0] on ELF; the TEB gs:[0x58] on PE) rides the MemOffset operand into the absdisp32.mem slot (mod=00 rm=100 + SIB index=none base=101 disp32-only — the 64-bit absolute-addressing form; a bare mod=00 rm=101 would be [rip+disp32]). This ONE row therefore serves ELF and PE with config-only differences — the target JSON stays format-blind. Opcode 0x8B = MOV r64, r/m64 (register form of the load); result rides ModR/M.reg. Pure read (no side effects); never a terminator.

variants.$comment: 1-op absolute-SIB form: `mov r64, seg:[disp32]`. Byte layout: payload(seg) + REX.W(0x48|R) + 0x8B + ModRM(mod=00 reg=dest rm=100) + SIB(0x25 = scale0 index-none base-disp32) + disp32 LE. The MemOffset operand supplies the literal disp32 (format config's tlsAccess.baseDisplacement).

opcodes.$comment: Load-effective-address. Operands: [base_reg, MemBase(scale), MemOffset(disp)] OR [base_reg, index_reg, MemBase(scale), MemOffset(disp)] OR [SymbolRef] (RIP-relative form, D-LK4-RODATA-PRODUCER 2026-06-02) OR [base_reg, SymbolRef] (TLS relocated-displacement form, D-CSUBSET-THREAD-LOCAL). AS1 emits x86_64 `lea reg, [base + index*scale + disp]` / `lea reg, [rip + sym]` / `lea reg, [base + tpoff32(sym)]`. Cycle 3c uses the 3-operand form for MIR Gep without an index; index-arm via 4-operand form; RIP-relative arm materializes a module-level global's address into a register for downstream loads/stores or function-pointer calls; the TLS arm adds the linker-patched thread-pointer offset to the tp register. Encoding: REX.W 0x8D /r ModR/M [SIB?] disp32. Opcode 0x8D is the `lea` mnemonic; the modes differ only in ModR/M.mod (10 for [base+disp], 00 for [rip+disp]).

variants.$comment: 3-op no-index form: `lea reg, [base + disp32]`.
4-op indexed form: `lea reg, [base + index*scale + disp32]`.

1-op RIP-relative form: `lea reg, [rip + sym]` (D-LK4-RODATA-PRODUCER 2026-06-02). The lone SymbolRef operand is wired to the RipRelDisp32 slot, which (a) records a rel32 Relocation at the trailing 4-byte position and (b) forces ModR/M to mod=00 rm=101 (the [rip+disp32] encoding in 64-bit mode). Used by `lowerGlobalAddr` to materialize a global's address into a register.

2-op block-address form: `lea reg, [rip + sym]` where sym is a SYNTHETIC per-block symbol (D-CSUBSET-COMPUTED-GOTO `&&label`). Byte-IDENTICAL to the 1-op RIP-relative form above — the SymbolRef (operand 0) wires to RipRelDisp32 with a rel32 reloc; the trailing BlockRef (operand 1) is METADATA, wired to NOTHING (it names the target LIR block so the assembler can bind `sym` to that block's byte offset). The assembler reads the BlockRef from the operand list and records a `BlockSymPatch{sym, targetBlock}`; the linker assigns `sym` the interior-block VA. Distinct guard `[symbol, blockref]` so the variant matcher routes this 2-operand `lea` here rather than failing A_NoMatchingEncodingVariant.

TLS C1 (D-CSUBSET-THREAD-LOCAL): 2-op relocated-displacement form: `lea reg, [base + tpoff32(sym)]` — REX.W 0x8D ModRM(mod=10 reg=dest rm=base) [SIB when base.lo3=100] + a 4-byte RELOC PLACEHOLDER at the memory-displacement position (Relocation{tls-tpoff32, sym}; the linker patches the SIGNED thread-pointer offset in). The base is the tp register `tlsbase` produced; the sum IS the thread-local object's per-thread address. Distinct guard [reg, symbol] — no other lea variant carries that shape.

opcodes.$comment: Sign-extend integer (cycle 3c cast lowering for MIR SExt). D-CSUBSET-LONG-PRIMITIVE closure (cycle 10h, 2026-06-04): I32→I64 variant landed. Per Intel SDM Vol. 2A, opcode 0x63 in 64-bit mode is unconditionally MOVSXD (in legacy mode it would be ARPL, but the assembler emits only 64-bit-mode code); REX.W=1 widens `r/m32 → r64` (the canonical I32→I64 sign-extend used here), REX.W=0 widens `r/m32 → r32` (effectively a mov). ModR/M.reg = dest (r64); ModR/M.rm = src (r/m32). Pairs with the next mnemonic's zext (byte→r64) — same single-variant shape. Other source widths (I8→I32/I64, I16→I32/I64) STILL not encoded, but the original blocking premise is GONE (audit-residue sweep c2 correction): FC3 c2 shipped the variant `guard.width` discriminator, so additional widths are now declarable without first-match-win hazards; the MIR→LIR SExt source gate keeps mis-width sources fail-loud meanwhile (D-TARGET-ENCODING-WIDTH-GUARD). The remaining variants are sub-native-width work — D-CSUBSET-SUBNATIVE-ALU-FORMS. Deferred until a c-subset corpus row actually emits an I8/I16→wider widening.

variants.$comment: width 32: I32→I64 movsxd r64, r/m32 (REX.W 0x63). Width-keyed (was width-absent) now that the byte form below is declared — convergence-fix D forbids mixing a match-any sibling with a width-keyed one. The MIR→LIR SExt arm threads widthFlagsForType(source): an I32 source → width 32 → here.

width 8 (D-CSUBSET-CHAR-STRING-VALUE-CODEGEN): Char/I8→int movsx r64, r/m8 (REX.W 0F BE /r). REX.W double duty (mirrors the zext byte form's 0F B6): (a) r64 dest — the low 32 are the sign-extended byte = the int result, consumed low-bits-only; (b) forces REX so the SRC low-byte register uses spl/bpl/sil/dil (a proper 8-bit access, not the legacy ah/ch/dh/bh alias). ModR/M.reg=dest, ModR/M.rm=src byte. Selected when the SExt source type is Char (width 8 on the LIR inst).

width 16 (D-CSUBSET-SUBNATIVE-ALU-FORMS): I16->int movsx r64, r/m16 (REX.W 0F BF /r). REX.W: (a) r64 dest — the low 32 hold the sign-extended halfword = the int result, consumed low-bits-only; (b) the r/m16 source reads EXACTLY the low 2 bytes, so a promoted I16 register's stale bits 31:16 are irrelevant. ModR/M.reg=dest, ModR/M.rm=src word. Selected when the SExt source type is I16 (width 16 on the LIR inst) — the short->int widening.

opcodes.$comment: D-LIR-SETCC-WIDTH-CONTRACT (step 13.5 cycle 1 post-fold, code-reviewer C2): zero-extend the low BYTE of a register to a full r64. Encoding `REX.W 0F B6 /r` — movzx r64, r/m8. ModR/M.reg = result (dest, 64-bit); ModR/M.rm = operand[0] (src, low-byte). REX.W=true serves double duty: (a) sets dest to r64, (b) forces REX prefix presence so the SRC low-byte access uses the spl/bpl/sil/dil aliasing (proper 8-bit access of r4..r7) instead of the legacy ah/ch/dh/bh high-byte alias. The natural consumer is the setcc → zext pair lowered by `lowerICmp`, but it is also reused by MIR ZExt for boolean-typed → int widening. FC3.5 sweep-c1 (D-CSUBSET-ZEXT-32-TO-64): the mnemonic now carries TWO width-keyed forms selected by the SOURCE type's width on the LIR inst (the MIR→LIR ZExt arm threads it): width 64 (the Bool/byte default — flags=0) keeps this movzx byte-widener byte-identically; width 32 (a U32 source) selects `mov r32, r32` below — a 32-bit register write zero-extends to the full r64 (Intel SDM MOV: 32-bit operands auto-zero the upper dword), which IS the U32→U64 conversion. Routing a U32 through the byte form would silently read ONE byte. Both variants are width-KEYED (no width-absent match-any), so an unforeseen width fails loud at the matcher instead of silently picking a wrong-width form.

variants.$comment: FC3.5: zext-from-U32 — mov r32, r/m32 (0x8B /r WITHOUT REX.W; the SAME bytes the `trunc` opcode emits, because the ISA operation is identical: a 32-bit register write zeroes bits 63:32). Two mnemonics deliberately share the encoding — each conversion polices its own inherent (source, dest) pair at the MIR→LIR gate.

width 16 (D-CSUBSET-SUBNATIVE-ALU-FORMS): U16->int movzx r64, r/m16 (REX.W 0F B7 /r). Reads EXACTLY the low 2 bytes, zero-extending into r64 — so a promoted U16 register's stale bits 31:16 are irrelevant (UNLIKE the width-32 `mov` form, which would copy them). ModR/M.reg=dest, ModR/M.rm=src word. Selected when the ZExt source type is U16 (width 16).

width 8 (D-CSUBSET-SUBNATIVE-ALU-FORMS): U8->int movzx r64, r/m8 (REX.W 0F B6 /r) — the SAME bytes as the Bool width-64 widener, but width-8-keyed (a U8 source). Reads exactly the low byte, zero-extending into r64; REX.W forces spl/bpl/sil/dil 8-bit access. Selected when the ZExt source type is U8 (width 8).

opcodes.$comment: Truncate integer (cycle 3c cast lowering for MIR Trunc). Operands: [src_reg]. FC3 c2 (D-CSUBSET-32BIT-ALU-FORMS): FIRST real encoding — the 32-bit-result form is `mov r32, r/m32` (0x8B /r WITHOUT REX.W): a 32-bit register write zero-extends to the full r64, which IS C's value mod 2^32 conversion (C23 6.3.1.3 for unsigned; the same bit pattern for the int window). The variant is deliberately WIDTH-KEYED 32: a Trunc to 16/8 bits (gated at MIR→LIR requireNativeIntWidth) that somehow reached the assembler would match NOTHING and fail loud (A_NoMatchingEncodingVariant) instead of silently emitting a 32-bit mov — belt + suspenders.

Bitwise AND. FC3.5 sweep-c2: FIRST encodings — AND r/m64, r64 = REX.W 21 /r and the no-REX.W 32-bit sibling (Intel SDM AND; the same 64/32 width-keyed reg-reg pair as add/sub). First consumer: the composed-FCmp materialization (x86 Oeq = sete ∧ setnp — lowerFCmp folds the two setcc+zext halves with `and`). Also makes source-level `&` live at both widths; xor/not stay un-encoded (the pre-existing assembler wall).

variants.$comment: and r/m32, r32 — 0x21 /r without REX.W (32-bit, zero-extends).

opcodes.$comment: Bitwise OR. FC3.5 sweep-c2: FIRST encodings — OR r/m64, r64 = REX.W 09 /r + the 32-bit sibling (Intel SDM OR). First consumer: the composed-FCmp Une materialization (setne ∨ setp).

variants.$comment: or r/m32, r32 — 0x09 /r without REX.W (32-bit, zero-extends).

opcodes.$comment: Bitwise XOR. Cluster-F F2 (core_bitwise): encodings added — XOR r/m64, r64 = REX.W 31 /r + the no-REX.W 32-bit sibling (Intel SDM XOR; the same 64/32 reg-reg shape as and/or, opcode 0x31 vs AND 0x21 / OR 0x09). Closes the source-level `^` assembler wall the bitwise_and_or example noted as deferred. 2-address: the r/m operand IS the destination (requires2Address legalize inserts the copy).

variants.$comment: xor r/m32, r32 — 0x31 /r without REX.W (32-bit, zero-extends).

opcodes.$comment: FC3.5 sweep-c1 (D-CSUBSET-32BIT-ALU-FORMS shifts residue): logical left shift — FIRST real encodings. Intel SDM SHL: the VARIABLE-count form `SHL r/m64, CL` = REX.W D3 /4 (the count is the IMPLICIT CL register — x86 has no 3-address reg-count shift); the CONSTANT-count form `SHL r/m64, imm8` = REX.W C1 /4 ib; 32-bit siblings = the same opcodes WITHOUT REX.W per the FC3-c2 width axis (a 32-bit shift auto-zero-extends its destination). Hardware masks the count to the operand width (& 63 / & 31, SDM Vol. 2B SAL/SAR/SHL/SHR) — C declares counts >= width UB (C23 6.5.7p3), so no extra masking is emitted; whatever the hardware does to a UB count is conforming. 2-address: the r/m operand IS the destination (requires2Address legalize inserts the copy). implicitRegisters (the FC1 idiv role-contract pattern): inputs=[rcx] (the core reads CL), inputRoles={count: rcx} — the MIR->LIR shift lowering pins the count vreg BY ROLE, never by name; clobbered=[rcx] expresses that the REALIZATION's count-pin mov destroys RCX's prior value across the sequence (regalloc's covered-position exclusion keeps live ranges out of RCX across the shift — the same mechanism that keeps div operands out of RAX/RDX). The exclusion is conservatively also active at imm-form positions (the constraint is per-OPCODE; costs one register of pressure there, never correctness). The lowering selects the imm8 variant when the count is a MIR Const in [0,255] AND a [reg,imm] variant is declared; otherwise the CL pair.

variants.$comment: SHL r/m64, CL — REX.W D3 /4. ONE explicit operand (the value/destination); CL is implicit.
SHL r/m32, CL — D3 /4 without REX.W.
SHL r/m64, imm8 — REX.W C1 /4 ib.
SHL r/m32, imm8 — C1 /4 ib without REX.W.

opcodes.$comment: FC3.5 sweep-c1: LOGICAL right shift (MIR LShr — unsigned operands; zero-fill). Intel SDM SHR: /5 digit on the same D3 (CL) / C1 (imm8) opcodes as shl. Same implicit-count role contract + width axis + UB-above-width note as shl.

FC3.5 sweep-c1: ARITHMETIC right shift (MIR AShr — signed operands; sign-fill). Intel SDM SAR: /7 digit on the same D3 (CL) / C1 (imm8) opcodes. Same implicit-count role contract + width axis + UB-above-width note as shl. C 6.5.7p5 leaves negative-operand >> implementation-defined; DSS defines it as SAR (sign-fill) on every target.

Bitwise NOT — 1-operand (one's complement). Cluster-F F2 (core_bitwise): encoding added — NOT r/m64 = REX.W 0xF7 /2 + the no-REX.W 32-bit sibling (Intel SDM NOT; same F7-group unary r/m shape as NEG = 0xF7 /3, modrmRegExt 2 vs NEG's 3). Closes the source-level `~` assembler wall. requires2Address mirrors neg/sub — the operand IS the destination; the 2-addr legalize inserts a mov when result != operand[0].

variants.$comment: not r/m32 — 0xF7 /2 without REX.W (32-bit one's complement, zero-extends).

opcodes.$comment: Integer two's-complement negate — REX.W 0xF7 /3 (NEG r/m64). D-CSUBSET-LOCAL-INT-CODEGEN closure (step 13.3b, 2026-06-02): encoding row added so c-subset's unary minus (`-x`) lowers through the assembler. requires2Address mirrors the sub/mul shape — the operand IS the destination, the 2-addr legalize pass guarantees this invariant before encoding (insert a mov when result != operand[0] at MIR→LIR time). modrmRegExt=3 fills ModR/M.reg with the /3 opcode-extension digit.

variants.$comment: FC3 c2: neg r/m32 — 0xF7 /3 without REX.W (32-bit two's-complement, zero-extends).

opcodes.$comment: Float arithmetic (cycle 3d FPR wave). FC2 Part B: ADDSD xmm1, xmm2 — F2 0F 58 /r (scalar double add). Mandatory prefix F2 selects the scalar-double form and is emitted BEFORE the auto-REX (xmm8-15 operands set REX.R/REX.B with rexW=false). 2-address like `mul`: dest goes to ModR/M.reg (op[0] IS the destination after lir_2addr_legalize), source-2 to ModR/M.rm. FC3.5 sweep-c2 (D-CSUBSET-F32-CODEGEN): the F32 sibling ADDSS xmm1, xmm2 = F3 0F 58 /r (Intel SDM ADDSS — same opcode bytes, the F3 scalar-single mandatory prefix) rides the width axis: the F64 variant is now width-KEYED 64, the F32 one 32 (an F32-typed MIR FAdd carries kLirInstFlagWidth32 via widthFlagsForType).

variants.$comment: FC3.5: ADDSS xmm1, xmm2 — F3 0F 58 /r (scalar single).

opcodes.$comment: Float subtract — SUBSD xmm1, xmm2 = F2 0F 5C /r (scalar double) / SUBSS = F3 0F 5C /r (scalar single), Intel SDM SUBSD/SUBSS. Cluster-F F3 (float_mul_sub): encoding added, mirroring fadd/fdiv (opcode 0F 5C vs fadd 0F 58). NON-COMMUTATIVE: dest=op[0] (the minuend) -> ModR/M.reg, op[1] (the subtrahend) -> ModR/M.rm, so `a - b` = reg(a) - rm(b) (same dest=ModR/M.reg convention as fdiv). 2-address (requires2Address): op[0] IS the destination after lir_2addr_legalize.

variants.$comment: SUBSS xmm1, xmm2 — F3 0F 5C /r (scalar single).

opcodes.$comment: Float multiply — MULSD xmm1, xmm2 = F2 0F 59 /r (scalar double) / MULSS = F3 0F 59 /r (scalar single), Intel SDM MULSD/MULSS. Cluster-F F3 (float_mul_sub): encoding added, mirroring fadd (opcode 0F 59 vs fadd 0F 58). Commutative; 2-address: dest=op[0] -> ModR/M.reg.

variants.$comment: MULSS xmm1, xmm2 — F3 0F 59 /r (scalar single).

opcodes.$comment: FC3.5 sweep-c2: float divide — DIVSD xmm1, xmm2 = F2 0F 5E /r (scalar double) / DIVSS = F3 0F 5E /r (scalar single), Intel SDM DIVSD/DIVSS. Same 2-address dest=ModR/M.reg shape as fadd (division is non-commutative: op[0] IS the dividend/destination after lir_2addr_legalize, op[1] the divisor in ModR/M.rm). First consumer: NaN construction (0.0/0.0) for the D-COND-FLOAT-NAN-TRUTHINESS-FCMP corpus witnesses — this also closes the fdiv encoding stub.

variants.$comment: FC3.5: DIVSS xmm1, xmm2 — F3 0F 5E /r (scalar single).

opcodes.$comment: FC3.5 sweep-c2 (FCmp LIR lowering): float compare writing FLAGS, no register result — the float sibling of `cmp`. UCOMISD xmm1, xmm2 = 66 0F 2E /r (scalar double) / UCOMISS xmm1, xmm2 = NP 0F 2E /r (scalar single — NO prefix; an F3 here would be an invalid encoding), Intel SDM UCOMISD/UCOMISS. Quiet (non-signaling on QNaN) compare: sets ZF/PF/CF — unordered → ZF=PF=CF=1, greater → all 0, less → CF=1, equal → ZF=1 (the truth table the float condCodeEncoding arms below realize; full derivation at mir_to_lir floatCmpPlan). ModRM roles: ModR/M.reg = operand[0] (the LEFT side of the predicate), ModR/M.rm = operand[1] — NOTE this is the RM-form direction, the MIRROR of the integer cmp's 0x39 MR-form wiring; the LIR-level convention (op[0] = left) is identical. Annex-F note: C's relational operators are SIGNALING comparisons (COMISD raises Invalid on quiet NaN); DSS does not model the FP environment (no fenv access), so the quiet UCOMI forms serve all predicates — the comparison RESULTS are identical, only the unmodeled FE_INVALID flag differs.

variants.$comment: FC3.5: UCOMISS xmm1, xmm2 — NP 0F 2E /r (no mandatory prefix).

opcodes.$comment: Float unary negate (the ABSTRACT MIR FNeg's LIR mnemonic). x86 has NO native fp-negate instruction, so this opcode carries NO encoding: `lowerFNeg` in mir_to_lir probes this opcode's declared-encoding presence (empty here) and takes the CAPABILITY fallback — mint a 16-byte rodata sign-mask + emit `fneg_mask` (xorpd/xorps reg, [rip+mask]) below. On arm64 the same-named `fneg` opcode DOES declare a native FNEG encoding, so the probe there emits it directly. Keeping this abstract-but-declared (rather than deleting it) preserves the MnemonicSlot::FNeg probe target that distinguishes the two realizations by capability (c78, D-CSUBSET-FLOAT-NEG-ENCODING).

c78 (D-CSUBSET-FLOAT-NEG-ENCODING): the x86 realization of float negate — XOR the value against a 16-byte rodata SIGN-MASK (bit 63 set for F64 / bit 31 for F32, high bytes zero). This is gcc's `xorpd xmm0, XMMWORD PTR .LC0[rip]`, chosen for IEEE exactness: XOR flips ONLY the sign bit, so -(+0.0)=-0.0, -(-0.0)=+0.0, and -NaN preserves the payload — the `0.0 - x` alternative gives +0.0 for x=+0.0 (wrong signed zero) and mishandles NaN. `lowerFNeg` mints an anonymous 16-byte, 16-byte-aligned CONST rodata global (a U128 whose low qword/dword holds the mask, emitted via the existing float-literal-promotion mint mechanism) and materializes its address, then emits this op with operands [value, maskSymbol]. 2-address (requires2Address): the destination xmm = ModR/M.reg (op[0], the value; the legalize inserts `movaps dst, value`), the mask is ModR/M.rm via the RIP-relative disp32 slot (mod=00 rm=101 + a rel32 relocation against the mask symbol — the SAME `riprel.disp32` slot `movsd_load`/`lea` use). SSE m128 requires the memory operand be 16-byte aligned at runtime — the mint's 16-byte alignment + the section-alignment layout guarantee it on ELF (sh_addralign) and PE (4KB sectionAlignment, a multiple of 16). F64 = XORPD 66 0F 57 /r (width 64); F32 = XORPS 0F 57 /r (NP, width 32).

variants.$comment: F64: XORPD xmm, [rip+mask] — 66 0F 57 /r. Mirrors the fadd/fmul 2-address shape (NO explicit resultSlot — the result register IS op[0], guaranteed by the requires2Address legalize's `movaps dst, value` + operands[0]:=result rewrite; wire index:0 writes ModR/M.reg = that register). op[1] (mask symbol) -> the riprel.disp32 slot (mod=00 rm=101 + a rel32 reloc against the mask symbol).
F32: XORPS xmm, [rip+mask] — NP 0F 57 /r (no mandatory prefix — a 66 here would be XORPD). Same operand roles + riprel mask as the F64 form.

opcodes.$comment: Float-to-float precision change (FPTrunc f64→f32, FPExt f32→f64). FC3.5 sweep-c2: encoded — CVTSD2SS xmm1, xmm2 = F2 0F 5A /r (double→single) and CVTSS2SD xmm1, xmm2 = F3 0F 5A /r (single→double), Intel SDM CVTSD2SS/CVTSS2SD: SAME opcode bytes, the mandatory prefix names the SOURCE width. The width axis therefore keys on the SOURCE: the MIR→LIR FPTrunc/FPExt arm threads a widthOverride from the source operand's type (the result-width default would select exactly the WRONG sibling — FPTrunc's F32 result would pick the F3 widen). ModR/M.reg = the xmm destination (resultSlot), ModR/M.rm = the xmm source.

variants.$comment: F64 source → CVTSD2SS (narrow to single).
F32 source → CVTSS2SD (widen to double).

opcodes.$comment: Float-to-signed-integer conversion (FPToSI). FC2 Part B: CVTTSD2SI r64, xmm — F2 REX.W 0F 2C /r (truncating scalar-double → signed 64-bit). Operand roles per Intel SDM: ModR/M.reg = the GPR DESTINATION (resultSlot), ModR/M.rm = the xmm SOURCE. REX.W widens the destination to r64 (the sign-extended result also serves i32 results — the backend's i32-in-r64 convention). FC3.5 sweep-c2: the F32-source sibling CVTTSS2SI r64, xmm = F3 REX.W 0F 2C /r (Intel SDM CVTTSS2SI — REX.W stays: the destination is still the 64-bit GPR). The width axis keys on the SOURCE float width (the MIR→LIR FPToSI arm threads a widthOverride from the source operand's type; the result-width default — an I32 result → 32 — would mis-key the source axis).

variants.$comment: FC3.5: CVTTSS2SI r64, xmm — F3 REX.W 0F 2C /r (truncating scalar-single → signed 64-bit).

opcodes.$comment: Float-to-unsigned-integer conversion (FPToUI). c78 (D-CSUBSET-FP-TO-UI-CODEGEN): for the c-subset's in-range needs (a double/float whose value fits the U32 result — sqlite's usage), the truncating signed CVTTSD2SI/CVTTSS2SI r64 form serves: any value in [0, 2^32) fits the signed-64 result exactly, and the U32 result reads the low 32 bits (gcc emits the identical `cvttsd2si rax, xmm0` for `(unsigned)d`). SAME encoding + operand roles + SOURCE-width axis as fp_to_si: ModR/M.reg = the GPR destination (resultSlot), ModR/M.rm = the xmm source; REX.W keeps the 64-bit destination. Full correct unsigned-i64 (a double ≥ 2^63 → the conditional-subtract sequence) is deferred as D-CSUBSET-UI-FROM-FP-UNSIGNED-I64; sqlite does not hit it.

variants.$comment: F64 source → CVTTSD2SI r64, xmm — F2 REX.W 0F 2C /r (truncating scalar-double → signed 64-bit; the low 32 bits are the in-range U32 result).
F32 source → CVTTSS2SI r64, xmm — F3 REX.W 0F 2C /r.

opcodes.$comment: Signed-integer-to-float (SIToFP) — the REVERSE of fp_to_si above. CVTSI2SD xmm, r/m = F2 [REX.W] 0F 2A /r (Intel SDM: scalar signed-int -> double). Operand roles MIRROR cvttsd2si SWAPPED: ModR/M.reg = the xmm DESTINATION (resultSlot — the result is FPR-class via the F64 MIR result type, regClassFor), ModR/M.rm = the GPR SOURCE (wire 0). The width axis here keys on the SOURCE INTEGER width (the MIR->LIR SIToFP arm threads a widthOverride from the source operand's int type): a 32-bit source -> the no-REX.W form CVTSI2SD xmm, r32 (width=32), a 64-bit source -> REX.W CVTSI2SD xmm, r64 (width=64). REX.W selects the 64-bit source-integer interpretation (NOT the dest float width — the dest is fixed F64/sd here). DESIGN NOTE (audit + qemu-arm64 scrutiny): the variant guard offers ONE width axis, but cvtsi2sd/ss needs TWO independent selectors — REX.W (source-int width) AND F2-vs-F3 prefix (dest-float width). The source-int axis MUST vary (i32 vs i64 both occur; REX.W wrong = reads the wrong register width), so it OWNS the width axis; the dest-float prefix is fixed at F2/sd (F64 destination), the dominant + sqlite-blocking case (i64 -> volatile double). int -> F32 (`float f = anInt`, ss/F3) has NO encoding this cycle and FAILS LOUD at the mir_to_lir SIToFP arm (a deferred narrow gap, D-CSUBSET-INT-TO-F32-CODEGEN); sqlite uses `double` only.

variants.$comment: CVTSI2SD xmm, r64 — F2 REX.W 0F 2A /r (64-bit signed source). The sqlite i64 -> double blocker.
CVTSI2SD xmm, r32 — F2 0F 2A /r (32-bit signed source, no REX.W).

opcodes.$comment: Unsigned-integer-to-float (UIToFP). x86 SSE2 has NO direct unsigned-int->float opcode; for the c-subset's needs (where the unsigned value fits the SIGNED range of the next-wider int — true for U32 promoted into a 64-bit cvtsi2sd, and the common case) the same CVTSI2SD path serves. The SOURCE-int-width axis matches si_to_fp: width=64 -> REX.W (a U64 whose top bit is set would mis-convert as negative — a documented x86 SSE2 limitation, not exercised by sqlite's u32/size_t-in-range usage), width=32 -> no-REX.W (a U32 source; cvtsi2sd of a 32-bit reg reads it as signed-32, but a U32 always fits the signed-64 result exactly, so the result is value-correct). Dest fixed F64/sd as in si_to_fp; U->F32 deferred (D-CSUBSET-INT-TO-F32-CODEGEN). Full correct-unsigned-i64 handling (the zero-extend + conditional-fixup sequence) is deferred as D-CSUBSET-UI-TO-FP-UNSIGNED-I64; sqlite does not hit it.

variants.$comment: CVTSI2SD xmm, r64 — F2 REX.W 0F 2A /r (64-bit source; see the unsigned-i64 caveat above).
CVTSI2SD xmm, r32 — F2 0F 2A /r (32-bit source; a U32 fits the signed-64 result exactly -> value-correct).

opcodes.$comment: Cross-class bit-pattern move (GPR↔FPR). AS1 emits x86_64 `movq` (the QWord variant). Cycle-3d Bitcast lowers via this opcode whenever src and dst register classes differ; same-class Bitcasts continue to use `mov` (handled by cycle 3c).

FC12b (D-FC12B-WIN64-VARIADIC-CALLEE): xmm→r64 bit-copy — MOVQ r/m64, xmm = 66 REX.W 0F 7E /r (Intel SDM MOVQ, the store-to-GPR direction). The CALLER of a Win64 variadic function duplicates each FP vararg into its matching integer (home) register so the callee's va_arg(double) can read it from the home slot. A SEPARATE opcode from `movq_xclass`: the variant guard keys only on (operandKinds, width) and CANNOT disambiguate the two MOVQ directions (both are `reg`/width-64), and `movq_xclass` is consumed bidirectionally by Bitcast — overloading it would force a silent miscompile on the GPR→FPR Bitcast direction. ModR/M.reg = the xmm SOURCE (the wired source operand → modrm.reg), ModR/M.rm = the r64 DESTINATION (the result vreg → modrm.rm). Byte-pin: `movq rdx, xmm1` = 66 48 0F 7E CA (66 prefix; REX.W=0x48; 0F 7E; ModR/M mod=11 reg=xmm1=001 rm=rdx=010 → 0xCA).

Intrinsic call. Payload = intrinsic id (per HirIntrinsicRegistry / MIR convention). Operands = args. Result is optional (some intrinsics produce values, some don't). AS1 maps the intrinsic id to its concrete sequence (e.g. memcpy, debugtrap, sqrt) per target. Cycle 3e lowers MIR IntrinsicCall to this opcode.

Spill reload pseudo-op. Payload = spill-slot index (LirSpillSlot.v). Result = the reloaded value's physical reg. The encoder/ML7 materializes this as `mov result, [stackPointer + slotOffset]`; the exact stackPointer + offset come from per-target callconv lowering. Target-blind at LIR tier.

Spill store pseudo-op. Payload = spill-slot index. Operand[0] = the physical reg whose value is being stored. The encoder/ML7 materializes this as `mov [stackPointer + slotOffset], operand`. Target-blind at LIR tier.

D-AS-REGALLOC-WIDE-CALL-OPERAND-COUNT (option E): outgoing-stack-arg store carrier. Emitted by the PRE-REGALLOC wide-call lowering pass for every outgoing call argument beyond the cc's register-passed capacity (config: argGprs/argFprs counts + slotAligned). Operand[0] = the arg VALUE vreg (still virtual pre-regalloc — regalloc assigns it, ending its live range HERE at the store instead of at the call, which is why a wide call no longer holds N register-operands). Payload = the arg's OVERFLOW SLOT INDEX (0-based, in outgoing-slot units, monotonic across the call's stacked args in source order — the NSAA cursor). lir_callconv materializes it (post-regalloc) into `store operand, [sp + shadowSpaceBytes + payload*outgoingSlotSize]` — BYTE-IDENTICAL geometry to the pre-existing post-regalloc placement-loop stack store, so the caller-store↔callee-load offset contract is unchanged. No encoding (materialized away). hasSideEffects=true so no pass drops/reorders it off its call.

FC2 Part B: FPR-class register-register copy — MOVAPS xmm1, xmm2 = 0F 28 /r (NO mandatory prefix; the F2-prefixed reg-reg MOVSD form merges into the destination's upper lane, creating a false dependency on dst's prior value — MOVAPS copies the full register, the classic codegen choice). ModR/M.reg = destination (resultSlot), ModR/M.rm = source; xmm8-15 set REX.R/REX.B automatically. Bound to the `fpr` class's `move` slot in registerClassOps[] — consumed by the 2-addr legalize implicit copy, MIR→LIR mov-to-fresh/phi-edge copies, and callconv arg/return moves whenever the register class is FPR.

FC2 Part B: FPR-class 8-byte load — MOVSD xmm, m64 = F2 0F 10 /r. Two variants: (a) the 3-op [base + disp32] form mirroring the universal `load` operand shape ([base_reg, MemBase(scale), MemOffset(disp)]) — what MIR Load-of-pointer lowers to (GlobalAddr → `lea reg, [rip+sym]` materializes the address, this loads through it); (b) the 1-op RIP-relative SymbolRef form mirroring `lea`'s riprel.disp32 variant (F2 0F 10 05 rel32 + rel32 relocation) — byte-pinned now, consumed end-to-end when a GlobalAddr+Load fold peephole lands. Bound to the `fpr` class's `load` slot in registerClassOps[]. The STORE form is `movsd_store` (next row — landed with its first consumer, the ms_x64 FPR saved-reg prologue spill). WIDTH-KEYED since FC3.5 sweep-c2 (D-CSUBSET-F32-CODEGEN closure): the F64 variants (F2, width 64) carry their F32 MOVSS siblings (F3 0F 10, width 32) directly below — see the encoding-level $comment. (Audit-residue sweep c2 corrected the prior 'F64-WIDTH ONLY … when an F32 consumer exists' tail, which the variants below had already contradicted.)

encoding.$comment: FC3.5 sweep-c2 (D-CSUBSET-F32-CODEGEN): width-keyed pairs — MOVSD xmm, m64 = F2 0F 10 (8-byte read, width 64) vs MOVSS xmm, m32 = F3 0F 10 (4-byte read, width 32; Intel SDM MOVSS). The MIR→LIR lowerLoad threads the loaded type's width for FPR-class results: an 8-byte movsd read of a 4-byte F32 rodata item would read past the item (potential fault at a section edge) — the width axis makes the access width type-exact. Both operandKinds shapes carry the pair (the loader's keyed/absent-mix rule).

variants.$comment: FC3.5: MOVSS xmm, m32 — F3 0F 10 /r (4-byte load).
FC3.5: the RIP-relative MOVSS form (F3 0F 10 05 rel32) — the F32 mirror of the F64 riprel variant (consumed end-to-end when the GlobalAddr+Load fold peephole lands; byte-pinned now).

opcodes.$comment: FC2 (PE float witness, 2026-06-10): FPR-class 8-byte store — MOVSD m64, xmm = F2 0F 11 /r. ModRM DIRECTION is the load's mirror (Intel SDM): 0F 10 loads (xmm ← r/m), 0F 11 stores (r/m ← xmm) — the ModRM ROLES are identical in both (reg = the xmm, rm = the memory operand); only the opcode's direction bit decides which one is read vs written. One variant: the 4-op [value_reg, base_reg, MemBase(scale), MemOffset(disp)] form mirroring the universal `store` operand shape exactly (value → modrm.reg, base → modrm.rm.mem — emitFrameStore's operand order works unchanged). First consumer: the ms_x64 prologue/epilogue saved-reg spill — Win64 declares xmm6-15 CALLEE-SAVED and regalloc prefers callee-saved, so the FIRST FPR vreg under ms_x64 needs this store (SysV has no callee-saved XMMs, never reached it). Bound to the `fpr` class's `store` slot in registerClassOps[]. The full 16-byte XMM is NOT preserved (Win64 requires only the low 64 bits of xmm6-15 to survive — MOVSD's m64 store IS the ABI contract; the regalloc SPILL path stays width-default 64 accordingly). WIDTH-KEYED since FC3.5 sweep-c2 (D-CSUBSET-F32-CODEGEN closure): the F32 MOVSS store sibling (F3 0F 11, width 32) sits directly below for type-exact value stores — see the encoding-level $comment. (Audit-residue sweep c2 corrected the prior 'F64-WIDTH ONLY … when an F32 consumer exists' tail, which the variant below had already contradicted.)

encoding.$comment: FC3.5 sweep-c2: width-keyed pair — MOVSD m64, xmm (F2, width 64) vs MOVSS m32, xmm (F3 0F 11, width 32; Intel SDM MOVSS). lowerStore threads the stored VALUE's type width for FPR-class stores (type-exact access width); the regalloc spill path stays width-default 64 over 8-byte slots (value-preserving for both float widths — the F32 value is the low 4 bytes either way).

variants.$comment: FC3.5: MOVSS m32, xmm — F3 0F 11 /r (4-byte store).

$registersComment: Physical register file. Ordinal = position in this array (consumed by `LirReg::id` post-regalloc). Only the registers ML6 regalloc + ML7 callconv reference are declared here — sub-byte aliases (al/ah/bl/...) are omitted until a real consumer needs them. Width is in bytes (8/4/2/1). hwEncoding = ModR/M ordinal the assembler/encoder will read.
$registerClassOpsComment: FC2 Part B: per-register-class move/load/store mnemonics. A class WITHOUT a row uses the universal defaults (mov/load/store) iff it is gpr — the substrate default class; every other row-less class FAILS LOUD at the consumer (a GPR mov against an XMM hwEncoding assembles to valid-looking-but-WRONG bytes — the class-blind silent miscompile this table kills). fpr's `store` landed with its FIRST consumer (trigger discipline honored): the ms_x64 prologue saved-reg spill — Win64 callee-saved xmm6-15 + regalloc's callee-saved-first policy put the first FPR vreg in a callee-saved xmm, and classOpHandle failed loud L_RequiredLirOpcodeMissing until movsd_store was declared.
$callingConventionsComment: Per-platform x86_64 ABIs. ML7 callconv lowering selects one based on driver flag / function attribute. SysV AMD64 = Linux/macOS/BSD; ms_x64 = Windows. argGprs/argFprs ordering is significant (caller MUST place int args in those registers in that order, then spill to stack).

callingConventions.name: sysv_amd64
callingConventions.$comment: System V AMD64 ABI (Linux, macOS, BSD). Integer args: RDI/RSI/RDX/RCX/R8/R9 then stack. Float args: XMM0..7 then stack. Integer return: RAX (low 64 bits) + RDX (high 64 bits if >64-bit aggregate). Float return: XMM0/XMM1. Stack 16-byte aligned BEFORE call (RSP%16==0); the call instruction pushes RIP, leaving RSP%16==8 at callee entry. 128-byte red zone below RSP for leaf functions.
callingConventions.$framePointerComment: D-CSUBSET-VLA (C1b): rbp is the frame-pointer register a VLA function reserves as a stable fixed-frame base (captured at the fixed-frame bottom, then every fixed-frame ref switches SP→rbp so it survives the runtime `sub rsp,<vlaSize>`). A NON-VLA function never reserves it — rbp stays an ordinary callee-saved allocatable GPR (byte-identical frames). Consumed by lir_regalloc (pool exclusion) + lir_callconv (force-save + prologue capture + epilogue restore).
callingConventions.$aggregateAbiComment: FC7 (D-FC7-STRUCT-BY-VALUE-ARG-RETURN): System V AMD64 §3.2.3 eightbyte classification — a struct/union ≤16 bytes is passed/returned in up to two registers (each 8-byte 'eightbyte' classified INTEGER→GPR or SSE→FPR per the field types overlapping it); larger than aggregateMaxRegBytes (16) goes in MEMORY (by-reference args / sret returns). No indirectResultRegister ⇒ the sret hidden pointer is a first INTEGER arg (rdi) returned in rax (vs AAPCS64's x8). The classification ALGORITHM lives in the aggregate_abi realization tier keyed on this strategy enum, never on cc.name/target identity.
callingConventions.$aggregateStackExhaustsComment: D-FC12-VARIADIC-OVERFLOW-FIXED-AGGREGATE-STACK-ARGS: System V §3.2.3 — when a by-value aggregate does not fit the remaining arg registers, 'the assignments get reverted' and it is passed wholly on the stack, but the leftover registers REMAIN AVAILABLE for a later (smaller) arg (gcc function_arg_advance_64 leaves cum->nregs untouched). false = BACKFILL — the per-class cursor is NOT advanced. This DIVERGES from AAPCS64 (true/exhaust); declared explicitly so the fact is visible in config, not buried in a default.
callingConventions.$entryStackPointerBiasComment: D-LK10-ENTRY-TRAMP-PROLOGUE: at SysV process entry the kernel maps the image and JUMPS to `_start` with RSP 16-byte-aligned and NO return address pushed — so RSP bias mod stackAlignment is 0. The trampoline emitter computes its prologue `sub rsp, N` via `alignedSizeWithBias(shadowSpaceBytes=0, stackAlignment=16, bias=0) = 0` — no prologue emitted, which is correct for SysV (no shadow space + already aligned).
callingConventions.$callPushBytesComment: D-LK10-ENTRY-ML7-FRAME-BIAS-UNIFY: x86_64 `call` instruction pushes the 8-byte return address. ML7 `computeFrameLayout` consumes this as the bias for non-leaf functions so their prologue `sub rsp, N` produces RSP ≡ 0 mod 16 at the next call site. SysV's entry bias is 0 (kernel JMP) but this in-program call-push is 8 — the two distinct facts coincide on Win64 (both 8) and diverge here. Without this wired, the user-fn → extern-call shadow-space + alignment violation reappears one stack frame below the trampoline (the same 0xC0000005 class as D-LK10-ENTRY-TRAMP-PROLOGUE, now recurring at the user-fn boundary).
callingConventions.$variadicVectorCountRegComment: D-LANG-VARIADIC (step 13.4, 2026-06-02): SysV AMD64 §3.5.7 — the caller MUST set AL to the number of vector (XMM) registers used to pass variadic arguments before any call to a function with `...`. printf reads %al to know whether to consult the XMM save area in its va_list walk; passing AL uninitialized yields garbage formatting and (on hardened glibcs) an abort. C-subset has no float type today, so every variadic call from c-subset stamps the count as 0; the count-of-FPRs logic in ML7 materialize is data-driven via this field — future float support (in c-subset or any other source language) lights up the same code path with zero substrate change. The field names `rax` (the register table's canonical full-width name) rather than `al` (the ABI document's low-byte alias) because the register table does NOT yet model the x86 subregister aliases (al / ax / eax for the low-byte / low-word / low-dword of rax); anchored D-TARGET-SUBREGISTER-ALIASES. Writing rax = count is ABI-equivalent: a `mov rax, <count<=8>` sets AL to the same byte and the upper bits are irrelevant to printf's ABI contract.
callingConventions.$vaListLayoutComment: FC12a-core (D-FC12A-VARIADIC-CALLEE): System V AMD64 §3.5.7 variadic-CALLEE layout. `__va_list_tag` = {u32 gp_offset@0; u32 fp_offset@4; void* overflow_arg_area@8; void* reg_save_area@16;} = 24B. A variadic callee's prologue spills the 6 integer arg regs (rdi..r9, 8B each = 48B) then the al-gated 8 SSE arg regs (xmm0..xmm7, 16B each = 128B) into a 176B register-save-area. va_start sets gp_offset = fixedGPR*8, fp_offset = 48 + fixedSSE*16, reg_save_area = &save, overflow_arg_area = &(incoming stack args). va_arg(T) of an INTEGER reads reg_save_area+gp_offset (bump +8) while gp_offset < gpOffsetLimit(48), else overflow; an SSE arg reads reg_save_area+fp_offset (bump +16) while fp_offset < fpOffsetLimit(176), else overflow. Every fact is config — the HIR→MIR diamond + LIR prologue spill read these, never branching on cc.name/arch/format. Win64/AAPCS64 omit this block (different variadic ABI) and fail loud at the va_start site.

callingConventions.name: ms_x64
callingConventions.$comment: Microsoft x64 ABI (Windows). First 4 args in RCX/RDX/R8/R9 regardless of int-vs-float (slot-aligned: an XMM arg in slot N uses XMM_N AND consumes the matching GPR slot, even if the GPR is unused; symmetrically, an integer in slot N consumes XMM_N). Integer return: RAX. Float return: XMM0. Stack 16-byte aligned before call; 32-byte shadow space (home space) always allocated by caller for callee to spill arg registers. No red zone.
callingConventions.$aggregateComment: FC7 C2 (D-FC7-STRUCT-BY-VALUE-ARG-RETURN): MS x64 by-value aggregates. A struct/union of size 1/2/4/8 (power of two ≤ 8) is passed/returned in ONE GPR (treated as an integer of its size — Win64 has no SSE/HFA rule); every other size (3/5/6/7 or > 8) goes BY REFERENCE: a caller-allocated copy for args, an sret hidden pointer (in RCX, arg slot 0) returning the pointer in RAX for results. `win64_by_size` drives the bounded `classifyAggregate` arm; maxRegBytes = 8 (a single register).
callingConventions.$framePointerComment: D-CSUBSET-VLA (C1b): rbp is the frame-pointer register a VLA function reserves as a stable fixed-frame base (captured at the fixed-frame bottom, then every fixed-frame ref switches SP→rbp so it survives the runtime `sub rsp,<vlaSize>`). A NON-VLA function never reserves it — rbp stays an ordinary callee-saved allocatable GPR (byte-identical frames). Consumed by lir_regalloc (pool exclusion) + lir_callconv (force-save + prologue capture + epilogue restore).
callingConventions.$entryStackPointerBiasComment: D-LK10-ENTRY-TRAMP-PROLOGUE: at Windows process entry, `RtlUserThreadStart` does a CALL into the entry point, so RSP at the first trampoline instruction is ≡8 mod 16. The trampoline emitter computes its prologue `sub rsp, N` via `alignedSizeWithBias(shadowSpaceBytes=32, stackAlignment=16, bias=8)`: smallest N >= 32 with N ≡ 8 mod 16 = 40. That single `sub rsp, 0x28` (a) reserves 32 bytes of caller-side shadow space for ExitProcess + (b) realigns RSP from ≡8 to ≡0 mod 16 so both `call user_entry` and `call [iat_ExitProcess]` enter their callees at the ABI-mandated RSP ≡8 mod 16. Without this field wired, ExitProcess's aligned-SSE stores produced STATUS_ACCESS_VIOLATION on CLI-emitted binaries (smoke test `mov eax,42; ret` hid it because the test fn never touched the stack and the specific ExitProcess code path didn't hit movaps with misaligned RSP).
callingConventions.$callPushBytesComment: D-LK10-ENTRY-ML7-FRAME-BIAS-UNIFY: x86_64 `call` instruction pushes the 8-byte return address (same as SysV — ISA-level fact).
